title: Joinery, Structural Thinking & Wood as Engineering Material

required.title: [Book]
required.author: Albert Jackson, David Day, and Simon Jennings

competence_markers.description: The student can cut a dado, rabbet, and mortise-and-tenon joint that fits without excessive force or visible gaps
The student has attempted dovetail joints (quality is developing; technique and understanding are the standard)
The student can trace the load path through a simple wooden structure and identify the weakest connection
The student understands wood movement and can explain how a tabletop or panel should be attached to allow for it
The student selects wood species based on the project's requirements and can explain their choice
The student applies an appropriate finish for the project's intended use (indoor vs. outdoor, wear surface vs. display)
The student has completed at least one piece of furniture that is square, level, stable, and finished to a standard they are proud of
The student designs the project on paper before beginning and follows the plan with minimal improvisation

content.why_this_matters:
At Stretching Branches you learned that wood is a real material and that you can make real things with it. Now you learn why wood has been the primary building material for most of human history — and why it still is for much of the world.

Wood is an engineering material. It has measurable strength, predictable behavior under load, and properties that vary by species, grain direction, moisture content, and how it is joined. A wooden bridge can span a river. A wooden frame can hold up a house. A wooden joint can last centuries if it is designed and cut correctly. The difference between a structure that stands for a generation and one that falls apart in a year is not the wood — it is the person who designed and built it.

In this packet, you will learn joinery that goes beyond nails and screws. You will cut dadoes, rabbets, mortise-and-tenon joints, and eventually dovetails — joints that derive their strength from geometry, not just fasteners. You will learn how forces travel through a wooden structure and why some configurations are strong and others are weak. You will select wood species based on their engineering properties, not just their availability. And you will build furniture of real quality — pieces that are square, solid, and worth keeping.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Joinery** — cutting joints that derive strength from geometry: dado, rabbet, mortise and tenon, dovetail
2. **Structural thinking** — understanding load paths, how forces travel through a frame, and why configuration matters
3. **Wood movement** — understanding that wood expands and contracts with moisture, and accounting for it in design
4. **Wood species selection** — choosing wood based on its properties for the task, not just availability
5. **Finishing as protection** — understanding that finishing is functional (preventing rot, wear, and degradation), not just cosmetic
6. **Design-to-execution** — translating a plan on paper into a physical object that matches it
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Joinery — Why Geometry Beats Fasteners

A nail or screw holds two pieces together through mechanical force — the fastener grips the wood fibers and resists being pulled out. This works, but it has limitations. The fastener is a point of stress concentration. The wood around it can split. The fastener can rust, loosen, or be pulled out under sustained load.

A well-cut joint holds through geometry. The shapes of the two pieces interlock so that the forces trying to pull them apart are resisted by the wood itself — not by a separate fastener. A mortise-and-tenon joint locks a projecting piece (the tenon) into a matching hole (the mortise). To pull the joint apart, you would have to break the wood. A dovetail joint interlocks two pieces with angled fingers that physically cannot be pulled apart in one direction without destroying the wood. These joints have held furniture and buildings together for centuries — long after any nail would have rusted away.

**The joints you will learn:**

**Dado joint:** A groove cut across the grain of one piece to receive the edge of another. Used for shelves fitted into a bookcase side. The shelf sits in the groove, supported along its entire width. To cut a dado: mark the width (equal to the thickness of the piece that will sit in it), mark the depth (typically 1/3 the thickness of the receiving board), and remove the material with a router, a dado blade on the table saw, or by making multiple passes with a hand saw and cleaning out with a chisel.

**Rabbet joint:** A step cut along the edge or end of a board. Used to recess the back panel of a cabinet, to join the sides of a box, or to create a lip for a lid. Like the dado, it increases glue surface area and provides mechanical support.

**Mortise and tenon:** The fundamental joint of all timber framing. A rectangular projection (tenon) fits precisely into a rectangular hole (mortise). When glued, this joint is extraordinarily strong because the tenon is surrounded by wood on all sides. The joint resists forces in every direction. To cut a mortise: drill out most of the waste and clean the walls square with a chisel. To cut a tenon: saw the cheeks (the flat faces) and shoulders (the perpendicular cuts that seat against the mortised piece) to precise dimensions. The tenon must fit snugly in the mortise — tight enough that it requires light tapping with a mallet to assemble, but not so tight that it splits the mortised piece.

**Dovetail joint:** The signature joint of fine woodworking. Angled pins and tails interlock so that the joint physically cannot be pulled apart along its axis. Used for drawer construction, box corners, and anywhere a joint must resist pulling force. The dovetail is the most demanding joint to cut accurately — the angles must match, the fit must be tight, and the surfaces must be flat. Hand-cut dovetails are a benchmark of woodworking skill. Do not expect your first dovetails to be beautiful. Expect them to be educational.

### Part 2: Structural Thinking — How Forces Travel Through Wood

When you sit on a chair, your weight does not just press on the seat. It travels through the structure. From the seat, through the joints that connect the seat to the legs, down the legs to the floor. Every connection in that path must be strong enough to carry the load. If any single joint fails, the chair fails.

This is a **load path.** Understanding load paths is the difference between building something that works and building something that collapses.

**Key principles:**
- **Compression** is force that pushes material together. Wood is strong in compression along the grain. A vertical post carrying a beam above it is in compression, and wood handles this well.
- **Tension** is force that pulls material apart. Wood is weaker in tension. A joint that must resist being pulled apart needs good joinery or strong fasteners — preferably both.
- **Shear** is force that tries to slide one part of the material past another. Wood has moderate shear strength along the grain and low shear strength across it. A shelf bracket that is screwed to a wall is resisting shear — the weight of the shelf is trying to pull the screws downward through the bracket.
- **Triangulation** is the structural principle that a triangle cannot be deformed without changing the length of one of its sides. This is why diagonal braces make structures rigid. A rectangular frame is wobbly — push on one corner and it collapses into a parallelogram. Add a diagonal brace and it becomes rigid. This principle governs everything from roof trusses to shelf brackets.

### Part 3: Wood Movement

Wood is a living material — or more precisely, it was alive, and it retains the cellular structure that made it alive. That cellular structure absorbs and releases moisture from the surrounding air. When humidity is high, wood absorbs moisture and expands. When humidity is low, wood releases moisture and contracts.

This movement is significant. A 12-inch-wide solid wood panel can expand or contract by 1/8 inch or more across the grain between summer and winter. If you build something that does not account for this movement, the wood will force the issue: panels crack, joints fail, surfaces buckle, drawers stick.

**Practical implications:**
- Solid wood panels (tabletops, cabinet sides) must be attached in ways that allow cross-grain movement. A tabletop screwed rigidly through a frame will crack.
- Plywood is dimensionally stable because its layers are oriented with alternating grain directions, canceling out movement. Use plywood where dimensional stability matters and solid wood where appearance and strength matter.
- Joints designed for solid wood must account for movement. A breadboard end on a tabletop (a strip glued across the end grain to keep it flat) must be attached so the tabletop can expand and contract while the breadboard holds it flat.

### Part 4: Wood Species and Selection

Choosing wood is an engineering decision. Different species have different properties, and the right choice depends on the application.

**Pine (softwood):** Light, easy to work, inexpensive. Good for: shelving, framing, practice projects, painted furniture. Not good for: surfaces that take abuse (it dents easily), outdoor use without treatment (it rots).

**Oak (hardwood):** Heavy, strong, durable. Excellent for: furniture, flooring, structural applications. The grain is pronounced and attractive. Red oak is porous (not good for outdoor use without careful finishing). White oak is naturally water-resistant (used for boats and barrels for centuries).

**Maple (hardwood):** Very hard, fine-grained, excellent for surfaces that take wear. Good for: cutting boards, workbench tops, tool handles. Difficult to work with hand tools because of its density.

**Walnut (hardwood):** Beautiful dark color, moderate hardness, excellent for fine furniture. Expensive. Good for: projects where appearance matters.

**Cedar (softwood):** Naturally rot-resistant and insect-resistant. Aromatic. Good for: outdoor projects (fences, decks, planters), closet lining. Light and soft — not structural.

For each project, ask: What forces will this piece experience? Will it be indoors or outdoors? Does appearance matter? What is the budget? The answers determine the species.

### Part 5: Finishing as Protection

At Stretching Branches, you learned basic finishing. At Weathering Storms, you understand finishing as engineering — protecting wood from the forces that destroy it.

**Moisture** causes rot, mold, and dimensional instability. Finish seals the wood surface, slowing moisture exchange.

**UV light** degrades wood fibers, turning them gray and brittle. Outdoor finishes must contain UV inhibitors.

**Wear** erodes the surface. High-traffic surfaces (tabletops, floors) need hard, durable finishes.

**Finishing options and their applications:**
- **Polyurethane** (oil-based or water-based): Hard, durable, water-resistant. Good for tabletops, shelves, anything that takes wear. Multiple coats with light sanding between.
- **Danish oil:** Penetrates the wood rather than sitting on top. Brings out the grain. Less protective than polyurethane but easier to apply and repair. Good for furniture that will be handled (the finish feels like wood, not plastic).
- **Exterior stain and sealer:** Pigmented finish for outdoor wood. The pigment blocks UV; the sealer blocks moisture. Essential for any outdoor project.
- **Paint:** Covers the wood completely. Maximum moisture protection. Hides imperfections but also hides the grain. Good for utilitarian pieces and outdoor projects.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Joint Quality
**Developing well:** Joints fit together with hand pressure or light mallet tapping. No visible gaps when assembled. Surfaces are flush. The student can cut the joint consistently — not just once by luck.

**Developing slowly:** Joints have visible gaps, require excessive force to assemble (indicating the mortise is too small or the tenon is too large — both indicate poor measurement), or fall apart without clamping or fasteners.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student measuring accurately before cutting? Are their tools sharp? Is the saw cutting on the waste side of the line? Most joint problems are measurement or marking problems, not cutting problems.

### 2. Structural Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can look at a piece of furniture and trace the load path. They can identify which joints bear the most stress. They can explain why a table wobbles (usually uneven legs or out-of-square joints) and how to fix it.

**Developing slowly:** The student builds structures without thinking about force distribution. They cannot explain why a joint failed or why a structure is unstable.

### 3. Wood Movement Awareness
**Developing well:** The student accounts for wood movement in their designs — attaching tabletops to allow cross-grain expansion, using plywood where stability matters, orienting grain correctly in panel construction.

**Developing slowly:** The student builds as if wood were dimensionally stable. Projects that crack or buckle seasonally indicate this gap.

### 4. Project Completion to Standard
**Developing well:** Projects are completed, finished, and evaluated honestly. The student can identify what they would do differently. The work is something they are willing to show others and use in their home.

**Developing slowly:** Projects are abandoned partway through, left unfinished, or completed to a standard the student knows is below their capability.
